chunk_summary: Tube weaning after acquired brain injury requires meeting criteria for safe oral intake, hydration, and weight gain, with staged weaning and instrumental testing to guide decisions and prevent complications like aspiration or dehydration.
learning_objectives: Understand criteria for tube weaning in children post-acquired brain injury.
Recognize the role of instrumental testing (VFSS/FEES) in feeding management.
Learn strategies for staged weaning to minimize risks of aspiration and feeding aversion.
key_facts: Tube weaning criteria include stable respiratory status, adequate oral hydration without aspiration, and weight gain over several weeks.
Instrumental testing (e.g., VFSS or FEES) is recommended when reintroducing thin liquids or questioning ongoing tube use.
Premature tube removal can lead to complications such as dehydration, aspiration pneumonia, and feeding aversion.
Staged weaning involves maintaining safe oral 'pleasure feeds' while ensuring hydration via tube feeding.
A 7-year-old post-hypoxic ABI aspirates thin liquids but tolerates purées and thick liquids without overt coughing.
reference_quote: "Instrumental testing (VFSS or FEES) is most useful when thin-liquid reintroduction is plausible or when ongoing tube use is being questioned."
document_key_topics: Tube weaning criteria after acquired brain injury
Role of instrumental testing in feeding decisions
Staged weaning strategies to prevent complications
